title: Food Coloring on White Cotton
stainChemistry: Food coloring is concentrated synthetic dye (Red 40, Blue 1, Yellow 5 / Tartrazine — FDA-approved azo / triphenylmethane dyes) in water + glycerin carrier. White cotton is the FORGIVING substrate for food coloring — tolerates the full toolkit (Dawn, OxiClean, 3% peroxide, sun, Rit Color Remover) without risk of stripping fabric dye (white = no fabric dye to strip). Heat is the only invariant: hot water / hot dryer permanently sets food coloring on white cotton just like it sets it on any cellulose fiber.
whyThisWorks: White cotton tolerates aggressive home chemistry safely (no fabric dye to lose). Cold flush + Dawn + OxiClean soak + 3% peroxide + sun + Rit Color Remover gives the full ladder. Heat is the only thing that locks food coloring permanently. Simple cotton garment / napkin / pillowcase = high success rate IF caught fresh.
defaultAssumption: White cotton T-shirt / napkin / kid clothes / pillowcase. Fresh = high success. Hot-dried = often permanent. White cotton is the easiest substrate for food coloring removal because it tolerates the full toolkit.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Flip inside out. Cold water through the BACK 1–2 min. Pulls fresh food dye out fast — most effective fast move.
Dawn into the spot with a soft toothbrush 5 min. Cool rinse. Repeat if color transfers to cloth.

spottingProtocol.equipment: OxiClean Versatile in warm water (NOT hot — under 100°F)
spottingProtocol.instruction: WHITE cotton only. Submerge stained area in OxiClean + WARM (NOT hot) water. 1–4 hours; overnight for stubborn. Oxidizes dye color on white cotton. NEVER hot water — sets food coloring.
Drop, dwell, blot. 3% peroxide directly on residual stain. Don't mix with bleach / ammonia.
Lay damp cotton in direct sunlight for hours. UV breaks down Red 40 + Blue 1 gradually. Multi-day exposure if needed.
Per label, sodium hydrosulfite. Strong sulfur smell — ventilate, gloves. Strips residual food dye. WHITE cotton only — strips ALL dye, so don't use on colored cotton (this card is white cotton only).
Cool wash per care label. AIR-DRY until stain is fully gone in daylight. ANY heat on residual food coloring = permanent.

steps: 3. OxiClean soak in WARM (not hot) water, 1–4 hr to overnight.
7. Cool wash + air-dry until fully clear.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold for initial; warm (not hot) for OxiClean; never hot wash; air-dry until clear
warnings: NEVER hot water — locks dye into cellulose
Rit Color Remover strips ALL dye — WHITE cotton only
Sulfur smell from Rit — ventilate, gloves
Speed matters most — fresh is winnable, hot-dried is permanent

homeSolutions.agent: White Cotton Is the Forgiving Substrate
homeSolutions.instruction: White cotton tolerates the full home toolkit on food coloring — Dawn, OxiClean soak, 3% peroxide, sun, Rit Color Remover. No fabric dye to lose, so aggressive home chemistry is safe. The only hard line: HEAT. Hot water and hot dryer permanently set food coloring. Cold/cool throughout, air-dry until visibly clear in daylight.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Back-Flush — The Most Effective Fast Move
homeSolutions.instruction: Flip inside out. Cold tap water through the BACK of the stain for 1–2 minutes. Most fresh food coloring rinses out fast on white cotton.
Dawn in cold water, soft toothbrush 5 min. Cool rinse. Repeat 2–3 cycles for concentrated colors (Red 40 is the toughest).

Fill a basin with WARM (not hot — under 100°F) water + 1 scoop OxiClean per gallon. Submerge the stained area. Soak 1 to 4 hours; overnight for stubborn stains. White cotton tolerates OxiClean well — oxidizes the food dye color.
Dab 3% drugstore peroxide directly on residual color. 5–10 min. Don't mix with ammonia or bleach. Rinse cool.
Lay the still-damp cotton in direct sunlight for several hours. UV breaks down Red 40 and Blue 1 gradually. Multi-day exposure for stubborn artificial dye.

homeSolutions.agent: Rit Color Remover — Nuclear Option for WHITE Cotton
homeSolutions.instruction: If color persists after sun + peroxide, Rit Color Remover (sodium hydrosulfite) at any craft / fabric store. Per label. Strong sulfur smell — gloves + ventilation. Strips residual food dye. White cotton only — would strip colored fabric.

homeSolutions.agent: Cool Wash, AIR-DRY UNTIL VISIBLY CLEAR
homeSolutions.instruction: Cool wash per care label. AIR-DRY in daylight. ANY residual food coloring + dryer heat = permanent. Once visibly clear, normal dryer is fine going forward.

steps: 1. Cold/cool throughout. NEVER hot water until fully clear.
3. Dawn + cold water + soft toothbrush, 2–3 cycles.
4. OxiClean soak in WARM (NOT hot) water, 1–4 hr to overnight.
7. Rit Color Remover (white cotton only, sulfur smell, ventilate).
8. Cool wash + AIR-DRY until visibly clear.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if hot water / hot dryer was used on residual food coloring (likely permanent), or if 3 home cycles + sunlight + Rit don't fully clear it.
warnings: HEAT SETS food coloring permanently — air-dry until visibly clear
NEVER hot water until fully clear
Rit Color Remover = WHITE cotton ONLY (strips colored fabric)
Sulfur smell from Rit — gloves + ventilation

neverDo: Never hot water on food coloring
Never hot dryer until fully clear
Never Rit Color Remover on colored fabric
fiberSensitivities: White cotton tolerates full toolkit (Dawn / OxiClean / 3% peroxide / sun / Rit)
Heat is the only invariant for food coloring
OxiClean warm not hot — water temp matters even on cotton

materialWarnings: White cotton is the forgiving substrate — full toolkit available
Rit is the home nuclear option (white only)

escalation.when: Hot water / dryer used on residual food coloring (likely permanent), 3 home cycles + sun + Rit don't fully clear, or expensive cotton garment with sentimental value.
escalation.whatToTell: "Food coloring on white cotton, color [Red 40 / Blue 1 / Yellow 5 / mixed], age [fresh / hot-dried], home-treated with cold + Dawn + OxiClean soak + 3% peroxide + sun + [Rit Color Remover]. No hot water until visibly clear. [Residual / hot-dried suspected]."

contextQuestions: How long ago — fresh / overnight / hot-dried?
Has it been through a hot wash or dryer?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Red 40 is the toughest variant. Hot-dried = often permanent. White cotton is the forgiving substrate — full home toolkit available. Concentrated soaks (kid stuck a bottle in their lap) need overnight OxiClean + multi-day sun.
